text: We rented 7 electric bikes on Fathers Day to cruise around Santa Barbara. We're not tourists, we live in the area but wanted to try something new and different. Highly recommend trying if you haven't already. It's a great way to see the beautiful city of Santa Barbara and at a good price too. Not only was it affordable but the staff were very helpful and knowledgeable about the sport. They made us feel comfortable and prepared for our adventure. They also have onsite parking and street parking available which makes there location pretty much perfect in our eyes(also minutes from the beach). Thanks guys for a memorable day! Highly recommend this company if your looking to rent bikes, especially electric bikes.

My husband and I rented two e bikes to ride around downtown Santa Barbara. Very easy to book online, affordable prices, we were able to arrange an hour before we wanted to rent the bikes. We rode around Montecito, butterfly beach, and downtown, and had a great time! Powerful and fun e bikes! Highly recommend! We rented in March 2021.

I've had this place bookmarked for quite some time! So when the Pump planned a birthday trip to Santa Barbara for me, I figured instead of seeing the seals we saw last time in Carpinteria, it would be great to see the butterflies this time! (And it was!)

If you come here, thinking that you'll be surrounded by butterflies flying all around you - you'll be disappointed because it doesn't quite look like that!

You'll see some butterflies flying about high above you but the majority of what there is to see is this - high up in the trees! Clusters of butterflies! 

This area is covered with butterfly clusters that are harder to see (and quite honestly look like leaves - but you can barely make out the pattern) but the trees and foliage are just storybook and breathtaking! I love it! And it's just so relaxing! 

And don't miss checking out the sea cliffs when walking out a little from the wooded butterfly grove! It's seriously breath taking! 

What a beautiful place to take in!

We parked at the black rock camp ground and started the hike from there. We got tickets (30 USD per vehicle) as we went to Ryan mountain the previous day, but no one was there to check our tickets when we arrived at black rock camp ground so maybe you can do it for free? Not sure, but I felt it was worth it to pay because it allows them to maintain the place.

We did the hike in march and the weather / temperature was perfect! The initial part of the hike was a bit warmer, it gets a bit windy at the top so would suggest to bring a windbreaker, it really helps. We start off with sandy terrains, and there are more greenery as you start to ascend (we did it anti clockwise). I think both ways are fine

You may feel like there isn’t much to see during the initial parts of the hike, but the view at the top is worth it, trust me! Overall the hike wasn’t too difficult even though it is rated as “challenging” in their official website. You could do without any equipments but we are in relatively good shape. Many shades along the way to take a break

On human traffic: We started at around 845am and there wasn’t a lot of people. For the first hour I think we were the only ones, and I preferred if there were more people but the crowds starting to come in around 10+am, so perhaps we started too early. Met around 20-30 people along the whole 3.5 hour journey. It was a privilege to be able to experience this :) enjoy!
Trail is wide and flat. I enjoyed sitting and just listening to the birds. I recommend coming in the morning, birds seem more active.

If you hate dogs then I don't recommend it, but I love dogs and find "The Wilcox Property" (as it's locally known) simply amazing. The sunset views are some of the best in the area and the dirt paths wind through the area with a natural grace you won't find in a highly manicured park.

There are multiple entrances to the park, on at the corner of Medcliff rd & selrose ln, another at the end of Mesa School Lane, and yet another at the intersection of Las Positas and Cliff Dr.

Nature area with a ton of hiking/biking trails. There are various trails for different fitness levels, and the maps indicate the level of difficulty. We took the Willow trail to the Bommer trail to Laurel Ridge trail for a large loop that was approximately 5.7 miles. The trails do have steep climbs at certain parts, but are moderate for the most part. The trails are mostly exposed with very little shade. The paths are wide enough for groups to exercise together, and I did note quite a few families with small children. You can see the ocean off in the distance and the breeze can be quite pleasant. The ground is very compact, although note that the Laurel Ridge trail coming down can be a bit technical.

Parking lots are available but they fill up quickly; it’s likely that you’ll need to wait for someone to leave or to try a different lot if you arrive past 9am on a weekend. Otherwise, you can park across the street on the highway, but he sure to read the posted signs. Credit cards are accepted at the pay station ($3 for parking). Porta-potties are available with foot pump sinks.  Overall, this place provides lots of opportunities to explore nature and get some fresh air.

Outstanding staff! We did a 2 night Discovery Dive for inexperienced but certified divers. The boat was the Truth. Not luxurious, but comfortable and everything worked. Clean bathrooms, hot showers, a dryer for your wet thing, a hanging area for your wet-suit. The food was very good and plentiful. The crew was always cheerful and incredibly helpful. This was a whole new experience for me. I had never been diving in cold water with a thick wet suit. It took awhile but I finally got the buoyancy under control, thanks to the knowledgeable crew who totally ignored my idiocy and just kept helping me. Great trip. Great experience.

This place is awesome, especially on a beautiful sunny day. Rose garden growing fast, soon to bloom. Wide open grassy space with a few benches and a few trees for some shade. Great place to play lawn games or have a picnic , or both. Right across street from great mission and church. Mission also has a great garden to walk through. Relaxing, family friendly.
This park is beautiful but the playground needs to be updated.

This beach is a nice place to sit and have lunch or even bbq since they have grills. It also has a cafe on the beach called shoreline Beach Café. It's a great spot to have breakfast on the beach literally on the beach toes in the sand and all. I highly recommend to anyone new to the area.

This place is really lovely. It's in the middle of the city, but it has is so much diversity of bird life. I don't even consider myself a huge bird lover, and I went on a whim, but the sounds these birds were making, and seeing them wade or fly around was amazing. It feels like you are somewhere much more remote, even though the sound of traffic is constantly in your ear. You can walk on a path around the whole place and end up the beach to make it a combo trip, then there's a cute French restaurant where you can have a glass of wine to wind down the day.

We spent the day with this amazing group on the beautiful Santa Cruz island. The whole day was breath taking. JD was patient, fun and informative! Everyone was professional from the harbor all the way back. These caves truly are breath taking and if you havent seen them please take time to do so...it's a time u wont forget. If you do please do it with these guys, they'll make it 100x better. Thanks again guys!

Small neighborhood park. They just remodeled it maybe two years ago to have new equipment, bark chips, etc. I love that it’s not accessible from Washington st. That helps keep it from being too busy.

My kids love the excavator and the swings, but also play tag on the ladders and slides. Picnic tables are present for snacks. There is a working water fountain. No dogs are allowed, but there are dog waste stations on the small sidewalk on the way into the park from Sagebrush.

The noise from Washington Street makes it very loud. All day. It is not a quiet, idyllic park.
Always nice to be at the beach and this is a nice quiet location. Clean and refreshing, fun and satisfying with great views.

Pretty little spot right off Hwy 243. It's well-maintained. There are picnic tables and grills to enjoy lunch or you can pick a spot somewhere along the edge of the lake to sit and enjoy. There is a round, wooden walkway/pier from which several people were fishing the day I was there. It extends over the water and is a great place from which to get an unobstructed view of the lake. I went on a weekday at noon. It was a little busy. It's a small enough place for that to happen easily. There was a group of young ladies playing music loud enough to be heard from the road. They were just having fun, but it detracted from the otherwise very peaceful setting. If I get back through the area, I'd like to try to visit on a weekday in the early morning or the evening. If you're in the area, it's definitely worth going to see, busy or not. The parking area is across the road from the lake and does require a wilderness pass, obtainable at a number of stores, including local convenience stores. Safe & fun travels.

The pirate park.
They close at sunset, but they have a small water park feature that kids love. The park has clean restrooms and is well kept. Its a beautiful place to bring your kids to play.

Coronado Beach in California is a beautiful, clean, and breezy spot — perfect for a relaxing day by the ocean. The sand is soft and well-maintained, and there’s plenty of open space to walk, lounge, or play a game of beach volleyball. It’s peaceful and scenic, with stunning views and a laid-back vibe.

What’s missing is a bit of convenience — there aren’t many washrooms, and there’s no boardwalk or much in the way of food stalls or snacks nearby. It’s best to come prepared.

Still, if you’re looking for a calm, clean beach experience without the crowds or chaos, Coronado Beach delivers. Just pack your own snacks and enjoy the fresh air!  Overall a good experience . 4 stars ⭐️ ⭐️⭐️⭐️

It’s a beach with sand!  The best thing to me is coming on a weekday when no one is here.  Parking is an issue as it is bordered by beach homes that are all practically connected to each other so only two real access points to the beach are readily available. Depending on the tide and wind you will find wooden debris strewn over the length of the beach but it is what it is.  It’s a fairly wide beach and the view is typical of SoCal but hits the spot of sitting in an upright beach chair on a cool and cloudy day with the good book ticks all your boxes.  Enjoy!

This trail is a good one. We got cellphone service on some parts of the trail, which is nice in case of an emergency. The main trail leads to multiple hikes. Just make sure you have a or have seen a map of the trail you are looking for. Google maps also has great images of the trails if you get lost. Otherwise it is kind of confusing.

May have to circle the parking lot a few times until a spot opens up, but great beach overall. Attentive lifeguards, plenty of space to spread out, bathrooms and rinse-off showers, and nice view of the coastline. There’s a pretty large grassy area, volleyball courts, and basketball courts. Visited many times on weekday evenings to skim board and catch the sunset. Parking lot is still pretty full around 6:00pm but usually we find a parking spot near the lot entrance or as someone is leaving. Weekends are more crowded and parking is trickier but if with patience, you’ll get a spot. If not, there’s another parking lot down the road (next to Belmont park) with a bigger lot and an additional lot across the street. From the times when I have visited, people at this beach were friendly and fun.
Well maintained hiking trail through some wild and scenic mountain wilderness. We went Winter hiking and had a great time. Never Hike Alone.
Tranquil trail surrounded by eucalyptus trees! Visited in January and there were lots of flocks of butterflies hanging from trees.

Carpinteria beach is ok.  The small beach has volunteers to keep it clean.  You need to pay $10 to park the vehicle for the day.  Also, shower rooms will ask for coin deposits.  The campground has RVs and tents.  It might be an eyesore considering how the Southern Cal may have lots of homeless.  The bathrooms are well kept. The walkway deck protects the dunes. There are better beaches.
Wonderful wide beach, attractive to both surfers and sunset watchers. Consider yourself lucky to find parking.
Beautiful. A perfect spot to watch the sunset no matter the time of the year - would highly recommend recommend to visit if you are in the area!

Easily hiking trail, and also it uses for mountain biking. some are moderately steep. Nature and small wild games are flourished across Topanga State Park.   You will see deer, rabbits, wildflowers, and rock formations.  Go there on a clear sunny day that you can see the Paciic Ocean.  Parking fee for day uses cost 10 dollars.

Really neat play structure aptly called Kids World is highlight of this park. Well shaded by beautiful large tree. Playground has tunnels, mazes, bridges, climbing apparatuses, swings and more. 4 year old refused to leave and all 3 kids worked up a good sweat from exploring and playing. Lots of kids of all ages but parents seemed nearby and was impressed seeing bigger kids watching out and not running over little ones. A good family friendly park even for an out of towner.
Short lovely walk along the Arroyo Burro Creek which you can hardly see because of growth.  Liked the fragrance of being "in the woods."

We did the Santa Ynez Valley Wine Tasting Tour and had such an amazing experience. Our tour guide was Tracey and she was the best. I proposed to my girlfriend at the second vineyard we went to and not only was it beautiful and a great moment, but Captain Jack's and Tracey helped me plan it out. It wasn't just the great service, but the trip itself was so much fun. They picked us up at our hotel, knew a lot about the area, took us to some great places that had great wine as well as an amazing lunch and olive oil tasting. We will definitely be going back soon. Just a great experience with people who cared and made sure that you were well taken care of.
This place is amazing. And never disappoints!!! The sunset's are always amazing. Rain or shine,  usually your neighbor's are friendly and it is a great place to just come and chill

Amazing spot!! Just be mindful they will tow if u stay too late, no overnight parking.. just re-park and Uber in .. late night soak is epic!!
(I think it's called lower hot spring trail on all trails and aka Montecito .. ps make sure u know when to cross the creek!!! )

Nice local nature area. Mostly flat and paved so easy hiking/bicycling. At the moment lots of water in the San Gabriel River due to recent storms so lots of running water and everything is nicely green. You can do a nice long hike either north towards the San Gabriels or South. A few bicycles, a couple of people on horseback and a few hikers. Plenty of parking at Whittier Narrows park.
Really nice for night walks, it gives me this old nostalgia I cannot explain.

My kids never wanted to stop surfing because of the great, fun instructors. Nick, another guy with curly hair and Ally literally pushed my sons for hours (not sure how they were able to do so for so long!). They never gave up on my boys and came
with a full force of energy and fun until all my kids learned to surf. It was an amazing experience. Ally did everything she could to make us feel comfortable and happy.
Absolutely beautiful, never seen anything like it! You MUST go here if you're ever in Santa Barbara!

I've camped at Carpenteria state beach and spent days enjoying the gentle surf and warm sand at the beach itself. It's one of my favorite places in California.

The beach is a long sandy arc dotted with tide pools. Dried kelp washed up from the huge beds offshore decorate the sand. A myriad of water birds keep company with families playing on the shore. Sometimes you can spot seals fishing in the dark, cold water not far from the surf. The weather is often foggy, and quite a bit cooler than inland. I should mention the off shore oil derricks and naturally occurring tar that can stick to you feet. Even with those annoyances, to me it's beautiful and idyllic. 

The campground is basically a 1920's parking lot. People and campers are packed together like sardines on the asphalt. The rangers are the grumpiest I've ever met. (But they keep the noise down, and with so many campers packed so closely together I think that's wise.) A train runs right past the campground intermittently all day and night. (Don't worry it's gated off.) Due to its popularity the campground is always in use and as a result probably doesn't look it's best, but none if that matters because you get to park your trailer or pitch your tent right next to the beach itself. Once I snagged a spot on the sand. It really was camping heaven. 
Parking is available on the street in town or you can pay to park all day in the large state park parking lot. There is a playground nearby in Linden street, and lots of places to eat a short walk away.

It's my favorite beach in California!

Stayed at the RV camp grounds-- full hook ups. Take note, it is not like a resort (no pool, no hot water without a quarter in the showers, no lights in the paths). It is a state park-- go to the beach and ocean water, tide pools, and tie each other up in the seaweed. Rustic but so enjoyable by the fire and family. Some members of our party went to the Motel 6 for 70$ (they're not campers) but drove over every day for the "experience." Parking fee is 10$ each day. If you are a disabled person, it's 50% off the camp site reservation. Show your disabled person season pass. Parking fee still applied.

Checked this place out today with the wife and kids. Awesome place to rent bikes and ride along the coast! Everything was contactless and super quick process and prices were very decent! Shout out to Conner! This dude was super helpful/friendly! Will definitely be coming back here.

Staying in Carpinteria for a few days and looking for some places to go to for walks and hikes. This place came up on yelp so we thought to go see it as it was of very close proximity and walking distance from where we are staying at. 

What a beautiful place it is. People complain in the reviews that it is not a real hiking spot. People should learn the difference between hiking and walking. 

Walking constitutes more of a simple and easier surfaces like the flat that you experience going to the bluffs. While when you hike, you need to go out and experience a more rugged terrain, exert more effort (uphills/downhills, rocks, dirt, gravel etc.)

The Carpenteria Bluff Nature Preserve is flat , paved and dirt combination and can be accessed from different trailheads like the Tarpit Park. 

There's so much to see like wildlife, flowers and views are amazing. There are many trail loops that people can go on for walking or running and these are all family friendly. There's a railroad track so be careful when crossing. 

The bluffs also leads you to the Seal Sanctuary. Many people will be there so maintain social distance and keep your masks on. There are days when you don't see the seals. That's ok. there are others things to do. Go down to the beach and take a stroll! I can't say enough about Carpinteria bluffs , the experience was one of the most memorable.

Great experience today at Uhaul Storage in Santa Barbara.  We were tired and sore from moving these last weeks...three in a row.  Finally needed to put the leftover stuff in storage.  Nathaniel greeted us so warmly and professionally, we felt like family.  He quickly got us what we needed and on our way in no time at all.  Nathaniel is a great example of why we keep coming back to Uhaul for our rental and storage needs.  Uhaul, he's a keeper!  Thank you again!!!

Well I knew it was near a freeway but I didn't think I was actually on it!  There was just a wall between us and the freeway was right there. Very noisy and did not sleep very well! When I arrived they couldn't find our reservation and we even had a number of the lot, luckily they had one left! I was not happy about that! Then the lots was so small our awning nearly touched the other RV. It's not in the nicest part of Santa Barbara either, the streets around us were a bit run down. I think they have a nerve to charge $70 a night. But they are the only RV park in Santa Barbara so they get away with it! The bathrooms were at least clean!

This trail rocks! I failed to reach the peak I got Lost! It's no joke people take care of each other out here. Google maps said the trail was closed and maybe for good reason the trials were somehow erased hard to find luckily fellow hikers have left small clues behind for others not to lose their way. Still I lost my way on the way down I followed what looked like a river once that flowed with water but was empty now which took me straight downward into the half way point of the trail i was suppose to be on the paths met between the 2 small mountains. The rest was easy as I remember the way back from there. Still I reccomend go with 2 or more people.

Love it, Love it, Love it.  Even on a foggy-ish day, it's beautiful here.  We get spoiled here, or maybe I just feel that way because I head home to MI in less than a week.  Loads of folks here using the park all of the time.  It's probably closed at sunset like most city parks but that wouldn't stop the walkers and joggers and cyclists and dogwalkers from strolling through.  Great location right near SBCC and the Mesa neighborhood, close enough to downtown.  Santa Barbara feels like a small town at a park like this, because there are lots of friendly folks and touristas snapping their photos, there are plenty of photo-friendly scenes so locals get in on that action too.

This lovely site has plenty of camping and activity space under beautiful,  mature oak trees.  There is a large on site kitchen with walk in fridge and small freezer, which is directly next to the covered gathering area.  This area has a small stage,  many picnic tables,  and lights to use at night.  The bathrooms were upgraded a few years ago,  and now include plenty of flush toilets and sinks with mirrors in a well lit structure. The open air showers are still there,  now with slightly more roof.  Really,  although it's cold this time of year (dress in layers,  CA people!), it's really one of the most delightful event spaces in the Santa Barbara area.

Nice little neighborhood park.  It's older but well kept. Mostly clean playground and not very crowded on a Saturday when we went.  No homeless people hanging around and felt safe over all.  I would recommend it if you live within walking distance. Probably not worth a long drive to get here however.

My sisters and I took my mother for her birthday and what a great experience we had!! The tour guide was great and very friendly!! He called us when he arrived at our hotel, than we picked up two more groups, not too far from our hotel. The tour got underway at 11 and ended at 5. Two wineries, than an olive oil and vinegar tasting (awesome!), lunch at a cute little Mexican restaurant, and our last winery. All the wineries were nice and very accomodating. The day went by so fast, but we never felt rushed leaving each spot, there was just enough time! The only thing I would have liked is to bring along some snacks like cheese, crackers, dips, but you cant really do that on this tour. Instead we just brought small bags of peanuts and chips that we carried in our purses. Overall, great time!!!

Great clean park. Dog clean up stations and big grassy area, but not fully gated so not a TRUE dog park. Good if your dogs listen though. Kids jungle gym & beach volleyball 🏐 set up. No bathrooms or water available.
It’s the woods. Dry and high. Definitely want something a little more rugged to get up to the camping site. Good dirt biking for small bikes and short rides.

Beautiful rock formation, felt being in another planet.  Must visit if you are visiting Joshua Tree. Expect to purchase a pass (roughly $30 per car) to enter the park unless you have it already.  10 to 15 mins walk, easy for kids as well,  you'll definitely spend good time taking pictures. It's COLD. Come prepared with good walking or hiking shoes, layers to protect from cold. Note that there are no public bathrooms.
Nice place for fitness enthusiasts who like training in the fresh air and 🌞

So cute. A little shady break from our walk along the arts and crafts fair. Bathrooms are nearby, though someone thought it would be fun to pee in the large shell-like structure.

Bench tables are available, but seems to lack some shade. May be difficult for smaller kids, but the 5+yr olds seem to enjoy it.  With my toddler, it was more of climbing some areas, without going through to many apparatuses.  Didn't really notice many swings around though too, but maybe I didn't look hard enough. Either way, it had pretty walkways and garden surroudnings.  Definately wish I has something nearby my home like this.
This park is really great! Tons of play options and swings and slides and tons of open space to run around and have a good time! Will be back!

This was a nice little loop that was perfect for something short and sweet if you're visiting the area! It's on a reservation and there is a fee to get in (about $15 per adult), the gates also close at 4pm, but it was worth it because the trails and roads are super well maintained it seemed. This loop had water running through and was really fun to go through and look around.

Accessible, beginner-friendly gym with helpful staff and a great community of regulars.

The emphasis here is definitely on bouldering. There are plenty of top roping routes, but be aware that only 4 of them support auto-belay. There are probably 3-4 times as many top rope routes that require a partner to belay.

It can get busy in the evenings, but I've never had trouble working in enough climbs to go home exhausted. I love it!

Really beautiful park! Highly recommend checking out the swimming hole at Rock Pool. Right now the water is really deep and you can jump from pretty high at your own risk. But there are lots of hidden rocks so make sure you check the depth of the area you plan on jumping into.

The water is a biturky and very cold and refreshing.

There's usually always people there so don't expect a nice private swimming hole but theres plenty of places along the creek you can go if you want more privacy.

The bike shop located in Isla Vista near UCSB. 

I regularly use both the University Bike shop and Varsity Bike Shop back and forth. Both great service but I try my best to utilize the shop as often as possible. The shop stand by their service and product. I especially like how they double-check the original part number when replacing parts in their shop.

Very pleasant park with wide lawn for kids to run. Small but good playground, a few covered picnic areas, a restroom, and an actual parking lot in case you want to unload the kids' tricycles or scooters or a big picnic spread. The view of palms and mountains is quite stunning. A very nice pocket park.

I visited here on 11/8/2020. When we visited most people people parked on the residential street to the right and walked up the hill. If you're not a local and just visiting for the first time, this trail is pretty hard to find. I saw no signs for markers
For the 7 falls trail. Make sure to do your research ahead of time or ask people along the way, as this trail connects to many others. We hiked for about an hour but we eventually turned around when we ran out of time. There was no sign of water yet in the lower streams. There is a long paved road that goes for awhile before you actually get to the dirt trails and it's mostly uphill. Beautiful views! Make sure you have enough time to finish the trails.

We and our dog loved this beach!  We have been hesitant to take him to a dog beach since they tend to be wide open and he likes to run off, but this beach felt safer since one side is a tall cliff wall and the other is the ocean.  He had a blast.  Lots of friendly dogs and people.  No poop in sight.  My dog isn't a swimmer but enjoyed playing with the other dogs as well as attempting to rock climb.  I highly recommend this dog beach!

One of the best beaches ever! Love going inside the little caves. Park on the main road for free. Watch for signs you’ll have to walk several minutes extra. $3 an hour parking or $10 all day for the lot.
Very pretty local Redlands park that had more to explore than I initially realized. I never knew there was a stage back there! Love the place for a nice walk or jog.

This campsite is only open to non-profits.  I attended a Cub scout campout here.  For San Diegans this place is extremely convenient.  For a weekend getaway with a pack of little ones the place is great.  You can see there was a lot of investment in this park.  Unfortunately the well system has struggled to remove microorganisms that make the well water not potable.  The bathrooms have operational flush toilets, but the sinks and camp taps have been taped off while they continue to work on getting the bromine treatment system to produce consistent results.  In the meantime the super friendly rangers help keep your jugs topped off.  All that said, just about anyone in San Diego could drive home and shower if so desired.

While being so close, it is nestled well into the canyon away from the sprawling housing and freeway noise.  The place just accommodates a single group, so little ones are free to roam the space without disturbing strangers.  There are day hikers passing through.  The trail is well maintained and brings you to a beautiful overlook above the reservoir.  Most of the trail is shaded beneath trees.  A great hike for small children.

There is a huge covered group dining area with an outdoor kitchen space.  No sinks or gas lines.  A good sized charcoal grill setup and lots of counter space.  There are plenty of picnic tables.  There is a 115V AC plug that may be accessed with an extension cord.  Requires ranger approval, I believe.

It's a beautiful space for a quick weekend outing with small children.  If only they could get that water system working correctly.
Thanks to Hector for making a memorable and safe trip!   Kayaking in Channel Island was unforgettable; beautiful Island, beautiful weather and nice people! Loved it so much! Thanks
Great place to take a walk and view the Pacific Ocean atop the bluff. Good view of the Harbor and Sterns Wharf.
Very nice and clean park very dog friendly

I've been on plenty of guided tours and my kayak excursion with Channel Islands Adventure Company ranks among the best! I received clear communication beforehand, and connecting with our guide once on the island was a piece of cake. Our guide, Chuck, had almost 20 years of experience guiding on the island, and it showed. His paddling instruction was easy to follow, as were his instructions once we hit the water. I felt comfortable with everything we did, even with very little ocean kayaking experience. Plus, it was fun to learn more about the flora, fauna, geology, and history of the island (and its waters), plus hear a few awesome stories of Chuck's own paddling in the area, which made the experience even more rich. I came away wanting to immediately book another trip - the sign of a fun day, and a grade A operation :)

We bought a 1 adult, 2 children Kayak Tour in Nov 2018. Total cost: $427 + separately $59/a and $46/c for the ferry tickets to get to the meeting point where the tour departs.

This trip was confirmed while we were on the road, booked one day prior to our arrival.
We made several phone calls to the hotline and asked questions about the itinerary.  Due to the high cost, we were on the fans to proceed. After calling to get as much details as we could over 2 different days and speaking with employees on the phone, we were led to believe we would not be able to see the painted caves unless we go with a tour like them or unless
1. we are guides ourselves who know the area well or 
2. we are very experienced class 4 white water rafters. 

We are competent kayakers who have kayaked in different parts of the world, but we're neither the above so reasons they gave us tipped our decision to buy the tour.
 
Although we lucked out on the nice weather and the guide was good, we were extremely disappointed because

1. we did not see the Painted Caves 
2. we did not spend 4 hrs kayaking 
3. Poor logistical setup and management

The ferry arrived on time and between waiting for our group of kayakers to gather and walking inland toward the Basecamp where equipment were, it took almost 20mins. I began to worry as I vaguely recalled the confirmation email indicated 10.30am tour begin, which is 15-30 mins after the ferry arrived.  We had not even suited up yet.

There was a meeting to split up the big group then an equipment briefing by our assigned guide.

After spending time suiting up, locking our stuff, we walked back towards the coast where the kayaks are. A 2nd meeting followed with an intro of everyone, safety briefing and kayak operations.  

No designated launch point/ ramp. 
The beach could be seen from the storage point but it's awkward and quite a distance to walk the kayaks over the soft sandy and rocky beach (and carry back after tour ends). I consider myself fit but there's no way I could carry the heavy double kayak by myself and with someone helping, I needed to pause multiple times which was when the guide and a kind customer offered to take over. Since this is a commercial outfitter, I would expect the business to have a roller cart to assist in the transport to minimize time and improve efficiency.

By the time we hit water, it was past noon.
After another round of water drills, we finally started the tour. Actual time spent on visiting the caves was less than 2 hrs.  

We spent a huge chunk of time running through briefings and logistics. We were grouped in a team with level "3" experience which is above average, meaning no one was inexperienced. I'd done a lot of kayaking and this was the longest ever prep time to get in the water - especially for only a small group of only 7. (I was in a kayak expedition with 40+, our prep time totaled less than 20mins). If it was summer, I would have melted in my wetsuits and PFD.
  
The problem:
I understand the importance of safety briefings but the Basecamp was such a long walk away which was a complete waste of precious time that could be spent on water. Note the walk is not far, it is just out of the way of the coastal activities and wasted relative to the limited time we had.  It does not matter if you are able-bodied military guys - as there is no way to cut down those time with the distance between the 4 points: ferry drop off, Basecamp, Kayak storage, launch point. Perhaps they had limitations by the Island regulations where to build their sheds but when we bought the tour, we were led to believe it was a 4-hour tour on water, not hour plus of walking around from point to point plus briefings. 

Essentially, I felt we paid $427 for a 2-hr guided tour.  There had been no breakdown of the itinerary on how the whole "trip" will be like. I felt short changed.

Half way through, we learnt from our guide that we weren't going to see the painted caves.I was shocked. My first reaction was perhaps weather conditions around those caves or the experience level of our group is a limiting factor. Then, he explained Painted caves are on the other far end of the island and only conducted during summer. I was crushed and now felt totally cheated.During the tour, another family shared with me they were also surprised when the guide mentioned we weren't going to the painted caves.

The caves the guide showed us were nice but we bought the tour to see the painted caves.  Yes, I wish we had more time to research and read every fine print on their website on my phone but we'd based our decision on the information given to us by the reps who knew we wanted to see the painted caves.  

The manager, Kim, followed up insisted we had great weather and saw nice caves. She missed the point. Had we known what we were going to get, I would not have paid $578 for a 2-hour kayak tour. We were misled and they were not picking up the responsibility of false and inaccurate information relayed.

Update: Have taken this so many times since my initial review and love this trail. This was initially to create a video for Mari Copeny aka LittleMissFlint for her 17th birthday.

Once you realize the path begins behind the sign, enjoy the path that winds through a community and offers amazing outdoor feel to Cherry Canyon.
Was there when it was cold and windy. Lots of sand flying around. Not the atmosphere that u see on videos from YouTube when it's nice outside.

Also called Hendry's beach this is a beautiful and flat beach, great for taking a stroll. I was looking for a new walking beach and a few years ago an employee at Lazy Acres market up the street highly recommended this beach which less than 5 minutes away from the market. 

Pros:  Free Parking and beautiful. 
There is free parking which is a plus and the parking lot is decently sized, I've never had a problem finding parking.  This beach is located just below the Douglas Family Preserve which is a beautiful and serene preserve of land with large Eucalyptus trees on a cliff overlooking the ocean with a dirt walking path that is opened to the public.

Cons: Occasional and unpredictable dog poop and tar.
So I do love this beach, and was ecstatic the first few times I visited, but there are some downsides to the beach.  First off if you have a dog this is a great place to walk them, there is one section of the beach for unleashed dogs and one for leashed dogs.  The downside is some owners let their furry friends poop in the water or even right on the sand without picking up after them which is just disgusting.  My husband evidenced both incidents on our last visit and dodging dog poop while walking and trying to enjoy made me never want to return.  I love to go barefoot when I walk on the beach and I shudder to think about stepping in dog crap or its remains, and frankly its the last thing I want to think about when walking on the beach.  Another downside but somewhat inevitable as most of the SB beaches have tar.  We spent literally 20-30 minutes removing each others tar from our feet after a less than hour visit to this beach.  I guess the simple solution would be to wear shoes here, but in my opinion that defeats the purpose of getting your toes in the water if you are going to the beach.

RUNNIN' WITH BUTTERFLIES!

A pasture, as far as the eye can visualize, the eco-richly filled with lush green grass, and butterflies flutter here and there. That's what I envision when I think about butterflies. The Monarch is the only butterfly known to make a two-way migration like birds and November through February is the time of year the Monarch butterflies migrate to Goleta. 

Free admittance, but the City of Goleta accepts donations to support the Monarch Butterfly Docent Program are welcomed. Open from sunset to sunrise, the 9.3-acre preserve includes Devereux Creek, woodlands and meadows as well as trails, an outdoor gathering area and classroom.
 
The grove is shaded with blue gum eucalyptus trees, the same place the Monarchs have returned to every year since the early 1900's. The number of butterflies visitors will see vary from year to year, ranging 20,000 to 500,000 and flutter around in the warmth of the sun.

If you're so inclined, after walking through the preserve, follow the trail to the buffs and enjoy the view of the Ocean. Better yet, head down the bluff and take a walk along the beach. If you like walking barefoot on the beach, be warned, there is tar on this beach, which is not so easy to get off. The upside, if you get tar on your clothes, WD40 is a removing agent, which may ruin some fabrics.
Such a good place in LA! A lot of surfers here! The waves are not very strong, so very suitable for fun surfing. I would really want to have a surf board since I came here. Will come back again!

This trail has seen better days. It's a pretty steep incline in a lot of areas where the trail has eroded away. The ruins of stairs and guide rails show where a safer trail used to be. The paths are pretty narrow at points, maybe down to 2ft wide. I didn't have any issue going up, but it's a workout. Coming down I lost my footing a few times in steeper areas. I didn't have gear but would recommend trekking poles and shoes with good traction. Zero shelter from trees so be sure to have water and sunscreen. The views are stunning. Id prob skip this one and opt for trails that are better kept.
Dirt hiking trail on the west and paved bike trail on the east.

Don't walk your dogs off leash here, they often will ticket you and it's silly how expensive that ticket is. Other than that, great beach!

In the Summer you will never find a parking space here as it becomes total tourist central. Can you blame them though? Carpinteria has the best weather in the summer, the most delicious burgers at The Spot, local tunes and brews at Island Brewing Co right on the train tracks, the Snow Cone trailer, volleyball courts, the platforms you can swim out to, dolphins leaping through the waves, and awesome shopping down Linden Ave. It's beach town heaven!

During the months that aren't flooded with people camping at the campgrounds and natural day trip tourists, I tend to visit Tower 3 & 4 most often because the parking lots are most empty and the beaches have more shells from the lack of people walking over that way. The waves are always small and the sand always feels great between your toes.

Because there is a tar pit so close by you will most likely get little speckles of tar on the bottoms of your feet at this beach. It's totally worth it thought and so easy to get off with oil. So walk the beach, play some volleyball, layout and tan, and view the beautiful lowkey waves while watching for dolphins riding into the unforgettable sunsets that occur here.
Beautiful park, in the morning i seen people enjoying a noce walk and exercise runs, but a couple transient guys hanging around not so good. But beautiful park has restroom and plenty of space

Had the best time biking around the beautiful Santa Barbara today. Only made better with the huge help of NATE. He got us all set up with our bikes and even recommended a great route for us. Thank you Nate!!!!

Bring a sweatshirt, as the tile and open space make this building always freezing cold on the hottest of days. A must see if visiting Santa Barbara. There are no words to describe the garden and old-Spanish architecture style of this historical site. I recommend all to stop on by.

Signed up for the electric bike tour, had a phenomenal time. Nate was the tour guide- 10/10. He did a great job. If you want to see a whole lot of Santa Barbara but aren't sure where to start, or don't want to spend more time than necessary researching, this is your place. The ebike tour covered quite a bit of ground. Would absolutely book again and am very much looking forward to the next trip.

This is a tough place to review because many of the negative reviews are based on the reality that this place is cramped, although based on our stay & observations several reviewers go way beyond reality to stereotypical Yelper hysteria.  The good and the bad ...

GOOD
* Location ... location, location, location!  This park is very central to downtown Santa Barbara.  I thought the surrounding area was fine, not fancy but never did we feel unsafe.
* Clean ... my experience at all time of the day was that the bathrooms and park in general were very clean
* Booking & check in ... booking online was easy, and they had our packet ready for us even though we arrived after hours.  They also communicated by email several times before and after our trip.  The website needs to say that there are no fires however
* Price ... look, its hard to say that $60 to $70 is a good price.  But most private RV parks are in that range, although yes most are more spacious.  You have to take into account this is Santa Barbara -- one of the most expensive places in an expensive state.  Check out motel prices in the area and the bottom line is that $60 was a big savings for us versus any other option on a holiday weekend.

BAD
* Location ... yes it's central but its directly adjacent to US-101.  There is a constant noise of traffic.  We were in an RV and we could not hear it at all inside.  That being said, if you are in an RV and want to leave the windows open all night ... or have a trailer/camper that will be open air, you need to understand that it is not a serene environment
* Cramped quarters ... I've posted some pictures but these are probably the smallest spaces I've ever parked in.  There's not a lot of privacy at any RV park but this is a whole new level.  In my view spots 7 & 8 are terrible ... they are awash in unshielded lights from the bathrooms all night long and are so close to the also unshielded bathroom doors.  Personally I think spaces 6, 7, 8, 15, 16 & 17 should be discounted very heavily.
* Hookups ... the cramped spaces lead to some very strange placement of hookups.  In some cases the sewer line is in downright odd or practically unusable spots.  

SUMMARY

I give this place four stars based on them working within the constraints. They really need to consider how to create some privacy around the bathrooms.  But I admit this is not a place I'd want to stay for an extended period of time or for an entire vacation.  If you are looking for a week's vacation in a beach destination, a two star review is reasonable based on the overly tight spaces & highway sound.
Nice little gem tucked away in residential neighborhood! Not crowded at all in fact you might have it all to yourself!
A well kept park on a very nice part of Irvine. Was there to watch a youth soccer game. The parking is kinda small, and I didn't see alot of places to eat nearby. Otherwise it was nice.

Absolutely love this park.
If you take your dog to the dog park section, please pick up after yourself. It’s horrible how much dog poop is there with no one picking up after themselves. Can’t even let your dog play with other dogs due to how much poop is there.

This is the most fascinating, intimate beach I've ever been to. It is private access, and I went with a friend, Arnie, who lives in the neighborhood above the cliffs, as well as with Nick, the friend I was staying with in Culver City. Not crowded at all. Only about 25 people on the whole beach, since the access is private. As you can see from the entry pic, you ain't getting in from the land access. You'll have to walk on the beach from a public access to get to use it. It was my first experience with the Pacific ocean, as well as my first time in California. I lost my footing once I got waist deep and the surf rolled me back up onto the beach like a fat, wet burrito. Even with that, the 20 minutes i spent on this beach will always be one of my fondest memories.

I never get tired of visiting this tree.  A city historical landmark, this tree is almost 300 years old. It's is so huge, it could no longer hold its branches up and they are down on the ground and the branches are larger than the trunks of most trees.  There are other beautiful trees on the property, but the Portola Sycamore is special.  And yes, you can climb the tree at your own risk.  This is one of those types of places my Dad would take us to when I was a kid and I'd wonder what all the fuss was about.  Go and take your kids and force them to look at the tree until they appreciate it as much as I do.  They'll thank you one day.

One of the big cultural highlights to any Santa Barbara trip. The old courthouse has outstanding, unique architecture that gives the city some real character. You can roam around the interior gardens and the outdoor collonades. There are free, guided tours that I highly recommend. You can also climb to the top of the bell tower to get some spectacular views of the city. Easily walkable from the main shopping district.
Great set of three group campsites nestled next to the ski slippers of Snow Valley. Great place to bring a group for a weekend of hiking, mountain biking and rock climbing.
Took my wife here today really really fun place to go the gentleman behind the counter even suggested a more affordable package so that we can get more for our dollar.

Reached the trailhead at 3:45pm on a Sunday in February. Easy to find parking. Did the "Warren Peak via Black Rock Trail and Panorama Loop" hike from allTrails. It came to 8 miles total with 1800 feet elevation gain and took me around 2h40m. I did the Panorama Loop counter clockwise and got to Warren Peak just 5 minutes before sunset. Definitely recommend it as a good sunset spot and also that you should carry a headlamp for getting back to the car from there in the dark. Trail is easy to follow. For Warren Peak, you need to climb 500 feet straight up in 0.7 miles, and even the high point of Panorama Loop is a decent climb to get to, so please carry plenty of water if attempting this in hot weather. Great views from both summits.

This is an urban oasis in that it is in the heart of LA, but is a open space park with steep hills, a few trails and affords some amazing views of downtown LA and nearby Highland Park and community. No amenities to speak of. (Didn’t see bathrooms). Others seem to be coming to get the steep climb exercise. Today it is super green with some native shrubs blooming

Super cute beach.  Very lowkey, and definitely has a private beach feel.  Enough sand to lay out a blanket, and chair, and relax.  Lots of seaweed gets tossed up & not reclaimed by the Ocean...but nothing intolerable. Tide pools on either end (very cool🤓) and rocky areas on either side, so be careful swimming (maybe use water shoes to be safe if swimming near the rocky ends).  Per usual in California, the water is FREEZING.  However it is very clean & was pleasant to dip my feet in while strolling along the edge of the ocean.  If you do swim, the waves are STRONG & powerful & it looks like rip currents are running thru. Also there is no lifeguard stand, so be safe & careful 🌊

Meanwhile, a lot of people have described the hill & stairs that bring you from the bluff to the beach, but I dropped in pictures so you can really see. Its not far at all.  However, you definitely want to put on hiking sandals/shoes or sneakers to walk down & back up.  Its not terrible tho...just, please avoid any slide type shoes/sandals.  Also if you have any knee or mobility issues, grab a hiking/walking stick to support you, and you'll be good!  The stairs & rail are sturdy, they just look intimidating.  Go slow, you'll be fine😊

As everyone has stated, parking is very interesting.  There's a tiny lot at the top of the bluff ($10/day) - otherwise you have to find parking along the PCH, then walk back to the park entrance.  (Watch for the "don't park" Signs!)

This was a great visit & definitely has been added to my list of fave Malibu beaches.  I can see myself coming here even after summer is over, just to sit, and write & meditate.  Zuma & Point Dume were my go-to's prior.  Super happy to have another to add to that list. 🙌🏾🥳

🚨Ps if you're preggers, if you drove to Malibu & drank your bodyweight in caffeine otw, or if you just in general have a finicky bladder, pick another beach - unless you're cool w/sprinting up 50-ish stairs + a quarter mile 75° incline + another 200 meters to get to the porta-potty🚻😬🥹 ...there are not any down on the beach.

Pretty cool place to visit during a super low tide. We came here just to check out the tide pools and they were really neat. We parked at the far right side parking lot and then walked from there downstairs to the other end and back. It was an easy walk and the views were really nice. The little walking path was also an easy walk and they have restrooms at each parking lot. For a little weekend day trip it was perfect. Next time we will pack a lunch and find a place to relax and eat. I would definitely recommend coming here on a low tide day. We will also come check it out in the summer because it was a really nice beach.

SB Adventure Company is in a class all its own. The only problem you face is deciding which adventure to choose as there are so many exciting options. 

We went with the wine tour by bike and we couldn't have been happier. The guides were very knowledgable about the area informing us on the drive up to the wineries about the unique geography of the Santa Ynez Valley...this was all new and exciting information to me...and I've lived in California my whole life. 

We stopped at 3 wineries and they were all beautiful. The bike ride in between was a great chance to view the scenery and get some fresh air. The ride was not strenuous and was actually very relaxing. The guides took very good care of us and provided us with fresh snacks and a generous deli spread for lunch. 

This experience definintely made me want to come back to the area and experience another one of SB Adventure Company's unique trips. We didn't want the experience to end! After we were all done, it felt as though we had known the 2 guides (Ryan and Joe) from way back and felt as though we had just gone on a trip with a couple buddies. 

A definite must do!

Rincon had waves and we enjoyed them for what they where at 2 to 3' iregular shape . We paddled alot which means we  surfed alot with long  100 yard rides.  Long rides= long paddling back out. After over a dozen rides it's time for my coffee so we proceed to get some.
Winter has been so bad that Rincon did not go off when it's typical to do so. So I can say that Rincon made my very happy today . 
Aloha
It was an awesome beach with surfing going on. And a lot of stuff to do. Saw beautiful parrots, shopping,, and eating as well. My favorite beach this trip.

Curious as to why this listing refers only to the plaza here at Chase Palm Park when it could just as well refer to the entire park (or when there could be a separate listing for the creek/pond behind the plaza).

As for the plaza itself, it's a nice, circular, amphitheatre-like venue with benches and walls around the circumference where folks can sit. The plaza gradually descends into a grassy lawn in the middle, complete with a decorative fountain. It can easily be used for public concerts and gatherings or private events and receptions. Indeed, there's a villa towards one side of the plaza that folks can rent for events.

Behind this villa is a beautiful creekside oasis, complete with footbridge for those event photo-ops. The park then extends further into a playground (covered separately here on Yelp). From end to end, it's a pleasant 10 minute walk. A space well worth exploring if you're not solely into the beaches here in Santa Barbara.

I had such a great time from beginning to the end! The process of booking online was very easy and clear. The boat ride to the island can be very adventurous and I recommend to bring extra warm or waterproof clothes. The kayaking guide I got was Dave, he was very attentive, try to respect each person in our group as well as other people enjoying the island. I also interacted some with Ben and another person (sorry I don't remember the name ) they were all very friendly, nice, knowledgeable and willing that the whole experience was just great. Thank you! I will definitely want to go back and try other type of tour.

One of my favorite beaches in SB. County,  go a little north of the campground,  sand is always clean,  good for swimming it's always calm,but of course cold, kind of a locals beach , where ever we travel we fit right in with the locals,  cool restaurants on the main drag.

We had a great time at the lake. The gentleman at the rental place was so kind! We rented a pontoon boat for an hour and just cruised around the lake before going on the hiking trails. Never knew there was so much to do here! You can rent pedal boats, kayaks, fishing boats too. Definitely a great way to spend a day when the beaches are overcast.
